name: Ka'ena Point Trail
tagline: Oahu's raw western tip — monk seals laze on rocks and albatross nest where the island ends.
parking: Free lot at end of Farrington Hwy past Yokohama Bay
hours: Sunrise to sunset daily (no gates on the Waianae side)
bestTime: Early morning to beat the heat; Nov–Jun for albatross nesting season
talkStory: In Hawaiian mythology Ka'ena Point is a leina a ka 'uhane — a sacred 'leaping place of souls' where spirits of the dead depart the physical world into the afterlife realm of Po. The trail follows an abandoned railroad bed built in 1899 by Benjamin Dillingham's Oahu Railway & Land Co., which once connected Honolulu to the North Shore around this rugged coast. The fenced sanctuary protects nesting Laysan albatross (moli) and critically endangered Hawaiian monk seals. Bring 2+ liters of water — zero shade for 5 miles, and the currents here are deadly.
